elements.sent-2411-001: We study the problem of robot navigation in dense and interactive crowds with environmental constraints such as corridors and furniture.
elements.sent-2411-002: Previous methods fail to consider all types of interactions among agents and obstacles, leading to unsafe and inefficient robot paths.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this article, we leverage a graph-based representation of crowded and constrained scenarios and propose a structured framework to learn robot navigation policies with deep reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-004: We first split the representations of different components in the environment, and propose a heterogeneous spatio-temporal graph to model distinct interactions among humans, robots, and obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-005: Based on the \hstGraph, we propose \modelName, a novel navigation policy network architecture with different components to capture heterogeneous interactions among entities through space and time.
elements.sent-2411-006: HEIGHT utilizes attention mechanisms to prioritize important interactions and a recurrent network to track changes in the dynamic scene over time, encouraging the robot to avoid collisions adaptively.
elements.sent-2411-007: Through extensive simulation and real-world experiments, we demonstrate that \modelName outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of success and efficiency in challenging navigation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, we demonstrate that our pipeline achieves better zero-shot generalization capability than previous works when the densities of humans and obstacles change.
elements.sent-2411-010: Robots are increasingly prevalent in human-centric environments.
elements.sent-2411-011: In applications such as last-mile delivery and household robots, the ability to navigate among humans is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, Fig.~\ref{fig:open shows a navigation scenario with abundant subtle interactions: Obstacles have a one-way effect on the paths of agents (i.e. humans and the robot), while the influence among agents is mutual.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Among agents, humans may react to other humans and robots in different ways.
elements.sent-2411-014: To navigate, a robot directly participates in some interactions in its close proximity, and simultaneously, is indirectly affected by other interactions.
elements.sent-2411-015: These interactions are heterogeneous, dynamic, and difficult to infer, making navigation in such environments challenging.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{Figures/open_figure.png} \caption{A heterogeneous graph aids spatio-temporal reasoning when a robot navigates in a crowded and constrained environment. The colored arrows denote \textcolor{red}{robot-human (RH)}, \textcolor{blue}{human-human (HH)}, and \textcolor{YellowOrange}{obstacle-agent (OA)} interactions. The opaque arrows are the more important interactions while the transparent arrows are the less important ones. At each timestep $t$, the robot reasons about these interactions, focuses on the important ones, and makes decisions.} \label{fig:open}
elements.sent-2411-016: Rising to these challenges, previous works have explored various approaches for robot crowd navigation~\cite{fox1997dynamic,van2008reciprocal,chen2019crowd .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, these works typically have one of two limitations: (1) They assume agents move in an open space without obstacles, which are common in the real-world~\cite{chen2017decentralized,chen2019crowd,yang2023st2 ; (2) They do not differentiate between various types of interactions, and thus the robot has difficulties taking adaptive strategies to avoid collisions with humans and obstacles~\cite{fox1997dynamic,chen2019relational,perez2021robot,liu2020decentralized .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Our goal is to navigate a robot to a destination without colliding with humans and obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-019: To solve this problem, we ask the following research question: How can a robot reason about diverse interactions in crowded and constrained environments to adaptively avoid collisions during navigation? To address this question, we propose a framework that takes advantage of the heterogeneity of interactions in crowded and constrained scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-020: First, we split the environment into human and obstacle representations, which are processed and fed separately into the reinforcement learning (RL)-based navigation pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-021: Then, we decompose the scenario into a heterogeneous spatio-temporal (st) graph with different types of edges to represent different types of interactions among the robot, humans, and obstacles, as shown in the colored arrows in Fig.~\ref{fig:open .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Finally, we convert the \hstGraph into a HEterogeneous Interaction GrapH Transformer (HEIGHT), a robot policy network consisting of different modules to parameterize the various spatio-temporal interactions.
elements.sent-2411-023: Specifically, we use two separate multi-head attention networks to address the different effects of robot-human (RH) and human-human (HH) interactions.
elements.sent-2411-024: The attention networks enable the robot to pay more attention to the important interactions, leading to a low collision-rate even as the number of humans increases, and the graph becomes more complex.
elements.sent-2411-025: In addition, we use a multilayer perceptron (MLP) to model the single-directional obstacle-agent interactions and a recurrent network for temporal evolution of the scene.
elements.sent-2411-026: In response to the rapidly changing scenario (Fig.~\ref{fig:open bottom), \modelName captures the heterogeneous interactions among different components through space and time, enabling the robot to avoid collisions and approach its goal in an efficient manner.}
elements.sent-2411-027: This article is expanded from a contribution on attention graph network proposed by our previous work~\cite{liu2023intention .}
elements.sent-2411-028: While our previous work focuses on crowd navigation in open spaces, this article incorporates static obstacles and constraints, which leads to significant modifications of scene representation and network architecture.
elements.sent-2411-029: Corresponding to these changes in methodology, new simulation and hardware experiments with new baseline comparisons are performed.
elements.sent-2411-030: In summary, the main contributions of this article are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: We propose an input representation of crowded and constrained environments that treats humans and obstacles differently.
elements.sent-2411-032: The split scene representation naturally allows us to inject structures in the rest of the framework.
elements.sent-2411-033: We propose a structured graph representation of crowded and constrained scenarios, named heterogeneous spatio-temporal graph (st-graph), to effectively model the pairwise interactions among all agents and entities.
elements.sent-2411-034: From the heterogenous st-graph, we use a principle approach to derive \modelName, a transformer-based robot navigation policy network with different modules to reason about all types of spatial and temporal interactions.
elements.sent-2411-035: The experiments in simulation with dense crowds and dense obstacles demonstrate that our method outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in unseen obstacle layouts.
elements.sent-2411-036: In addition, our method demonstrates better generalization to out-of-distribution environments with different human and obstacle densities.
elements.sent-2411-037: We successfully transfer the robot policy learned in a low-fidelity simulator to challenging real-world, everyday crowded environments without finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this section, we first review the literature of robot crowd navigation, which is divided into model-based and learning-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-039: Then, we discuss previous crowd navigation efforts in constrained spaces.
elements.sent-2411-040: Finally, we review graph attention mechanism with a focus on the usage of attention networks in multi-agent interaction modeling.
elements.sent-2411-041: Robot navigation in human crowds is particularly challenging and has been studied for decades~\cite{fox1997dynamic,hoy2015algorithms, savkin2014seeking,mavrogiannis2021core .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Model-based approaches have explored various mathematical models to optimize robot actions~\cite{van2011reciprocal, van2008reciprocal,fox1997dynamic,helbing1995social,huber2022avoiding,mavrogiannis2023winding .}
elements.sent-2411-043: As an early example, ROS navigation stack~\cite{ros_navigation_stack uses a cost map for global planning and dynamic window approach (DWA) for local planning~\cite{fox1997dynamic .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: DWA searches for the optimal velocity that brings the robot to the goal with the maximum clearance from any obstacle while obeying the dynamics of the robot.
elements.sent-2411-045: By treating humans as obstacles, DWA exhibits myopic behaviors such as oscillatory paths in dynamic environments~\cite{dobrevski2024dynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-046: As a step forward, optimal reciprocal collision avoidance (ORCA) and social force (SF) account for the velocities of agents.
elements.sent-2411-047: ORCA models other agents as velocity obstacles and assumes that agents avoid each other under the reciprocal rule~\cite{van2011reciprocal, van2008reciprocal .}
elements.sent-2411-048: SF models the interactions between the robot and other agents using attractive and repulsive forces~\cite{helbing1995social .}
elements.sent-2411-049: However, the hyperparameters of the model-based approaches are sensitive to crowd behaviors and thus need to be tuned carefully to ensure good performance~\cite{long2018towards,dobrevski2024dynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-050: In addition, model-based methods are prone to failures, such as the freezing problem, if the assumptions such as the reciprocal rule are broken~\cite{trautman2010unfreezing, liu2024relaxing .}
elements.sent-2411-051: In contrast, while our method also models these interactions, we learn the hyperparameters of the model from trial and error with minimal assumptions on human behaviors posed by model-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-052: Learning-based approaches have been widely used for navigation in dynamic environments to reduce hyperparameter tuning efforts and the number of assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-053: One example is supervised learning from expert demonstrations of desired behaviors, where the expert ranges from model-based policies~\cite{long2017deep,tai2018socially,xie2021towards , human teleoperators in simulators~\cite{pokle2019deep , to real pedestrians~\cite{karnan2022scand,chandra2024towards .}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Supervised learning does not require explorations of the state and action spaces of the robot, yet the performance of learned policy is limited by the quality of expert demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-055: Another line of work takes advantage of crowd simulators and learns policies with RL.
elements.sent-2411-056: Through trial and error, RL has the potential to learn robot behaviors that outperform model-based approaches and expert demonstrations~\cite{xie2023drlvo .}
elements.sent-2411-057: For example, Deep V-Learning first uses supervised learning with ORCA as the expert and then uses RL to learn a value function for path planning~\cite{chen2017decentralized,everett2018motion,chen2019crowd,chen2020robot_gaze,yang2023st2 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: However, Deep V-Learning assumes that state transitions of all agents are known without uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-059: In addition, since the networks are pre-trained with supervised learning, they share the same disadvantages with OCRA, which are hard to correct by RL~\cite{liu2020decentralized .}
elements.sent-2411-060: To address these problems, more recent efforts leverage model-free RL without supervised learning or assumptions on state transitions~\cite{liu2020decentralized,mun2023occlusion,liu2024relaxing,yang2023st2 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Since the state transition probability of humans is uncertain, directly learning a policy network without explicitly modeling state transitions is more suitable for navigation~\cite{sathyamoorthy2020densecavoid .}
elements.sent-2411-062: However, the aforementioned RL works have at least one of the following two problems: (1) They focus on navigation in open spaces and isolate agents from static constraints, posing difficulties when deploying robots in the real-world; (2) They ignore all or part of interactions among agents, which are important for robot navigation in dense crowds and highly constrained environments.
elements.sent-2411-063: We discuss how prior works address the above two problems in Sec.~\ref{sec:related_constrained and Sec.~\ref{sec:related_attn respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Besides dynamic agents, real-world navigation environments usually consist of static constraints, such as walls, furniture, and untraversable terrains.
elements.sent-2411-065: To deal with these constraints and humans, some methods such as DWA~\cite{fox1997dynamic and DS-RNN~\cite{liu2020decentralized use groups of small circles to represent the contours of obstacles.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: While groups of circles is a straightforward representation of obstacles, as we will show in Sec.~\ref{sec:sim_exp , they are not scalable as the number of obstacles increases and can lead to overfitting problems for learning-based approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Other learning-based approaches use raw sensor images or point clouds to represent the whole environment~\cite{perez2021robot,dugas2022navdreams,pokle2019deep,zheng2022hierarchical .}
elements.sent-2411-068: These end-to-end (e2e) pipelines have made promising progress in simulation.
elements.sent-2411-069: However, generalizing these e2e methods to real-world scenarios is challenging due to domain gaps such as inaccurate human gait simulation~\cite{mavrogiannis2023core,raj2024rethinking .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Despite the recent advancements in crowd navigation simulators~\cite{tsoi2022sean2,puig2024habitat,Kstner2024Arena3A and datasets~\cite{martin2021jrdb,karnan2022scand,hirose2023sacson , learning a deployable e2e policy that outperforms human teleoperation in dense and interactive crowds remains an open challenge.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Furthermore, prior works that demonstrate strong real-world performance in dense crowds and obstacles usually leverage processed inputs such as detected human states and processed sensor readings~\cite{sathyamoorthy2020densecavoid,xie2023drlvo,yang2023rmrl .}
elements.sent-2411-072: As such, we develop a structured input representation of humans and obstacles, which splits human and obstacle representations and feeds processed states to the policy network.
elements.sent-2411-073: This input representation leads to strong performance in both simulation and real-world.
elements.sent-2411-074: In contrast to the above works with processed inputs that focus on pedestrian behavior prediction~\cite{sathyamoorthy2020densecavoid , reward design~\cite{xie2023drlvo , and incorporating risks into map~\cite{yang2023rmrl , our paper proposes a principled way for network architecture design from the structures of complex navigation scenarios.}}}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Graph attention networks for multi-agent interactions
elements.sent-2411-075: In recent years, attention mechanisms have demonstrated success in various fields~\cite{vaswani2017attention,velickovic2018graph,vemula2018social .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Vaswani et al. propose a transformer with self-attention mechanism that achieves state-of-the-art performance in machine translation~\cite{vaswani2017attention .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Later, graph attention networks show the effectiveness of attention on learning relationships of data with graphical structures such as citation networks~\cite{velickovic2018graph .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Inspired by these works, researchers in trajectory prediction and crowd navigation have found that attention networks are also well-suited to capture interactions amongst agents and entities, which contain essential information for multi-agent tasks~\cite{vemula2018social,huang2019stgat, chen2019crowd, liu2020decentralized,hasan2022meta .}
elements.sent-2411-079: For each agent, these works compute attention scores for all neighboring agents.
elements.sent-2411-080: Due to the permutation invariance property, attention scores better capture the importance of pair-wise relationships than combining the information of all agents with concatenation or an LSTM encoder~\cite{chen2017decentralized,everett2018motion .}
elements.sent-2411-081: More specifically, in crowd navigation of robots and autonomous vehicles, a line of works uses a robot-human attention network to determine the relative importance of each human to the robot~\cite{chen2019crowd, leurent2019social, liu2020decentralized,liu2021learning .}
elements.sent-2411-082: However, interactions among humans, which can also influence the robot, are not explicitly modeled.
elements.sent-2411-083: To this end, other works use a homogenous graph network to include both RH and HH interactions~\cite{chen2019relational, liu2024sample .}
elements.sent-2411-084: However, since these works feed RH and HH features to a single attention network, the resulting robot policy has difficulty reasoning the specificity of each type of feature, which limits the robot's ability to adapt to different interactions, as demonstrated in \cite{chen2024socially and our experiments in Sec.~\ref{sec:sim_exp .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: In addition, the works above only deal with open-world social navigation and thus ignore the interactions between agents and obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-086: To this end, Chen et al. treat the humans, the robot, and static objects as different types of nodes in a heterogeneous graph attention network~\cite{chen2024socially .}
elements.sent-2411-087: The main difference from our work is that Chen et al. focus on semantic navigation, where a robot must navigate to an object in simulation.
elements.sent-2411-088: Our work focuses on point-goal navigation in crowded and constrained real-world environments.
elements.sent-2411-089: This difference leads to (1) Different representations of obstacles: the object representation in Chen et al. comes from a known semantic map of the environment, which is hard to obtain in the real-world.
elements.sent-2411-090: Thus, in our case, treating all obstacles as a 2D point cloud is more suitable for studying collision avoidance; (2) Chen et al. learn a value function and plan paths assuming simplified dynamics for agents, whereas we learn a model-free RL policy and assume unknown state transition for all agents, which is more suitable for sim2real transfer where the dynamics of pedestrians and robots are uncertain.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/pipeline.png} \caption{An overview of our pipeline in simulation and real-world. (a) At each timestep $t$ in training and testing, the simulator provides a reward $r^t$ and the following observations of the environment: obstacle point cloud $o^t$, the robot state $w^t$, and the human states $h_1^t, ..., h_n^t$, and masks $M^t$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:method_attn}). These observations serve as inputs to \modelName, which outputs a robot action $a^t$ that maximizes the future expected return $R^t$. The simulator executes the actions of all agents and the loop continues. (b) The testing loop in the real-world is similar to the simulator except the perception modules for obtaning the observations are different and the reward is absent. } \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-091: In this section, we formulate constrained crowd navigation as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and introduce the scene representation and the reward function.
elements.sent-2411-092: We model the constrained crowd navigation scenario as a MDP, defined by the tuple $ \langle \mathcal{S , \mathcal{A , \mathcal{P , R, \gamma, \mathcal{S _0 \rangle$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Let $w^t$ be the robot state which consists of the robot's position $(p_x, p_y)$, velocity $(u_x, u_y)$, goal position $(g_x, g_y)$, and heading angle $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Let ${h _i^t$ be the current state of the $i$-th human at time $t$, which consists of the human's position and velocity $(p_x^i, p_y^i, u_x^i, u_y^i)$.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Let ${o ^t$ be the current observation of the static obstacles and walls, which is represented as a 2D point cloud.}
elements.sent-2411-096: We define the state $s^t \in \mathcal{S}$ of the MDP as $s^t=[{w ^t, {o ^t, {h ^t_1, ..., {h ^t_n]$ if a total number of $n$ humans are observed at the timestep $t$, where $n$ may change within a range in different timesteps.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The humans are sorted by an increasing $L_2$ distance to the robot.
elements.sent-2411-098: The state space $\mathcal{S}$ is continuous and our choice of state representation is expanded in Sec.~\ref{sec:state_rep .}
elements.sent-2411-099: In each episode, the robot begins at an initial state $s^0\in \mathcal{S _0$.}
elements.sent-2411-100: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline (a), according to its policy $\pi(a^t|s^t)$, the robot takes an action $a^t\in\mathcal{A}$ at each timestep $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The action of the robot consists of the desired translational and rotational accelerations $a^t = [a_{trans ^t, a_{rot ^t]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: The action space $\mathcal{A}$ is discrete: the translational acceleration $a_{trans}\in\{-0.05\,m/s^2, 0\,m/s^2, 0.05\,m/s^2\}$ and the rotational acceleration $a_{rot}\in\{-0.1\,rad/s^2, 0\,rad/s^2, 0.1\,rad/s^2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-103: The translational and rotational velocity is clipped within $[-0.5\, m/s, 0.5\, m/s]$ and $[-1\, rad/s, 1\,rad/s]$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-104: The robot motion is governed by the dynamics of TurtleBot 2i.
elements.sent-2411-105: In return, the robot receives a reward $r^t$ (see Sec.~\ref{sec:reward for details) and transits to the next state $s^{t+1}$ according to an unknown state transition $\mathcal{P (\cdot|s^t, a^t)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Meanwhile, all other humans also take actions according to their policies and move to the next states with unknown state transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-107: The process continues until the robot reaches its goal, $t$ exceeds the maximum episode length $T=491$ steps, or the robot collides with any humans or static obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-108: The goal of the robot is to maximize the expected return, $R^t=\mathbb{E [\sum^T_{i=t}\gamma^{i-t r^{i ]$, where $\gamma$ is a discount factor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The value function $V^\pi (s)$ is defined as the expected return starting from $s$, and successively following policy $\pi$.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{Figures/scene_representation.png} \caption{A split representation of a crowded and constrained navigation scenario. In a dynamic scene, (b) low-level human states are detected by sensors and perception modules. (c) To obtain obstacle information, we remove all humans and compute a point cloud from a known map and the robot's location. In this way, we can learn a robot policy with a small sim2real gaps using a cheap low-fidelity simulator.} \label{fig:scene_rep}
elements.sent-2411-110: In our MDP, a state consists of a large and varying number of agents.
elements.sent-2411-111: To aid policy learning with such a complicated state space, we develop a structured representation of states that will be fed into the structured policy network.
elements.sent-2411-112: To reduce sim2real gaps caused by raw sensor readings, our scene representation leverages processed information from perception, maps, and robot localization, which are relatively robust to domain shifts.
elements.sent-2411-113: In Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_rep , at each timestep $t$, we split a scene into a human representation ${h ^t_1, ..., {h ^t_n$, and an obstacle representation ${o ^t$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: In human representation (Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_rep (b)), the position and velocity of each human are detected using off-the-shelf human detectors~\cite{Jia2020DRSPAAM,yolov3,deepsort .}}
elements.sent-2411-115: By representing each human as a low-dimentional state vector, we abstract away detailed information such as gaits and appearance, which are difficult to simulate accurately~\cite{tsoi2022sean2,mavrogiannis2023core .}
elements.sent-2411-116: We use a 2D point cloud as the obstacle representation.
elements.sent-2411-117: Instead of obtaining the point cloud from sensors, we take advantage of simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) and first create a map of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-118: During navigation, in Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_rep (c), assuming robot localization on the map is known, an ``artificial'' point cloud that only contains static obstacles is computed from the map.}
elements.sent-2411-119: Compared to real-time point clouds from sensors, our obstacle representation is not affected by the presence of humans and thus is less sensitive to inaccurate human simulations.
elements.sent-2411-120: The tradeoff is that our method requires a map and accurate localization.
elements.sent-2411-121: If a high-fidelity simulator is available, the ``artificial'' point cloud can be replaced by a real-time point cloud from sensors without changing the rest of our method described below.
elements.sent-2411-122: Our reward function consists of three parts.
elements.sent-2411-123: The first and main part of the function awards the robot for reaching its goal and penalizes the robot for colliding with or getting too close to humans or obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-124: In addition, we add a potential-based reward to guide the robot to approach the goal: \begin{equation}\label{eq:reward} \begin{split} \begin{gathered} r_{main}(s^t, a^t) = \begin{cases} 20, \text{if } d_{goal}^t \leq \rho_{robot} -20, \text{else if } d_{min}^t \leq 0 d_{min}^t - 0.25, \text{else if } 0<d_{min}^t<0.25 4(d_{goal}^{t-1}-d_{goal}^t), \text{otherwise}. \end{cases} \end{gathered} \end{split}\end{equation} where $\rho_{robot}$ is the radius of the robot, $d_{goal ^t$ is the $L2$ distance between the robot and its goal at time $t$, and $d_{min ^t$ is the minimum distance between the robot and any human or obstacle at time $t$.}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:reward}  \begin{gathered} r_{main}(s^t, a^t) =  20, if  d_{goal}^t \leq \rho_{robot} -20, else if  d_{min}^t \leq 0 d_{min}^t - 0.25, else if  0<d_{min}^t<0.25 4(d_{goal}^{t-1}-d_{goal}^t), otherwise.  \end{gathered} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-125: The second part is a spinning penalty $r_{spin}$ to penalize high rotational velocity: \begin{equation}r_{spin}(s^t, a^t) = -0.05||\omega^t||_2^2\end{equation} where $\omega_t$ is the current rotational velocity of the robot.
elements.sent-2411-126: Finally, we add another small constant penalty $r_{time =-0.025$ at each timestep to encourage the robot to finish the episode as soon as possible.}
elements.sent-2411-127: In summary, the reward function of our MDP is the sum of the above three parts: \begin{equation}r(s^t, a^t) = r_{main}(s^t, a^t) + r_{spin}(s^t, a^t) +r_{time}\end{equation} Intuitively, the robot gets a high reward when it approaches the goal with a high speed and a short and smooth path, while maintaining a safe distance from dynamic and static obstacles.

elements.sent-2411-128: In this section, we present our approach to decompose the constrained crowd navigation scenario as a heterogeneous st-graph, which leads to the derivation of the \modelName architecture in a structured manner.
elements.sent-2411-129: The subtle yet highly dynamic interactions among agents and entities are important factors that makes crowd navigation difficult.
elements.sent-2411-130: To model these interactions in a structured manner, we formulate the navigation scenario as a heterogeneous st-graph.
elements.sent-2411-131: In Fig.~\ref{fig:st a, at each timestep $t$, our \hstGraph $\mathcal{G ^t = (\mathcal{V ^t, \mathcal{E ^t)$ consists of a set of nodes $\mathcal{V ^t$ and a set of edges $\mathcal{E ^t$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: The nodes include the detected humans $h_1^t, ..., h_n^t$ and the robot $w^t$.
elements.sent-2411-133: In addition, an obstacle node $o^t$ represents the point cloud of all obstacles as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-134: At each timestep $t$, the spatial edges that connect different nodes denote the spatial interactions among nodes.
elements.sent-2411-135: Different spatial interactions have different effects on robot decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-136: Specifically, since we have control of the robot but not the humans, RH interactions have direct effects while HH interactions have indirect effects on the robot actions.
elements.sent-2411-137: As an example of indirect effects, if human A aggressively forces human B to turn toward the robot's front, the robot has to respond as a result of the interaction between A and B.
elements.sent-2411-138: Additionally, since the agents are dynamic but the obstacles are static, interactions among agents are mutual while the influence of static obstacles on agents is one-way.
elements.sent-2411-139: Thus, we categorize the spatial edges into three types: HH edges (blue in Fig.~\ref{fig:st ), obstacle-agent (OA) edges (orange), and RH edges (red).}
elements.sent-2411-140: The three types of edges allow us to factorize the spatial interactions into HH, OA, and RH functions.
elements.sent-2411-141: Each function is parameterized by a neural network that has learnable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-142: Compared to previous works that ignore some edges~\cite{chen2019crowd,liu2020decentralized,liu2023intention , our method allows the robot to reason about all observed spatial interactions that exist in constrained and crowded environments.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Since the movements of all agents cause the visibility of each human to change dynamically, the set of nodes $\mathcal{V ^t$ and edges $\mathcal{E ^t$ and the parameters of the interaction functions may change correspondingly.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: To this end, we integrate the temporal correlations of the graph $\mathcal{G ^t$ at different timesteps using another function denoted by the purple box in Fig.~\ref{fig:st (a).}}
elements.sent-2411-145: The temporal function connects the graphs at adjacent timesteps, which overcomes the short-sightedness of reactive policies and enables long-term decision-making of the robot.
elements.sent-2411-146: To reduce the number of parameters, the same type of edges in Fig.~\ref{fig:st (a) share the same function parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-147: This parameter sharing is important for the scalability of our graph because the number of parameters is kept constant when the number of human changes~\cite{jain2016structural .}

elements.sent-2411-173: At each timestep $t$, the simulator provides all state information that constitutes $s^t$, which is fed to the \modelName network.
elements.sent-2411-174: The network outputs the estimated value of the state $V(s^t)$ and the logarithmic probabilities of the robot's action $\pi(a^t|s^t)$, both of which are used to compute the PPO loss and then update the parameters in the network.
elements.sent-2411-175: In training, the robot action is sampled from the action distribution $\pi(a^t|s^t)$.
elements.sent-2411-176: In testing, the robot takes the action with the highest probability $a^t$.
elements.sent-2411-177: The robot action $a^t$ is fed into the simulator to compute the next state $s^{t+1}$, and then the loop continues.
elements.sent-2411-178: Without any supervised learning, our method is not limited by the performance of expert demonstrations~\cite{chen2017decentralized,chen2019crowd,liu2024sample .}
elements.sent-2411-179: However, to improve the low training data efficiency, an inherent problem of RL, \modelName can also be trained with a combination of imitation learning and RL.
elements.sent-2411-180: We present a structured and principled approach to design the robot policy network for crowd navigation in constrained environments.
elements.sent-2411-181: By decomposing the complex scenario into independent components, we split the complex problem into smaller functions, which are used to learn the parameters of the corresponding functions.
elements.sent-2411-182: By combining all components above, the end-to-end trainable \modelName allows the robot to perform spatial and temporal reasoning on all pairwise interactions, leading to better navigation performance.
elements.sent-2411-183: In this section, we present our environment, experiment setup, and results in simulation.
elements.sent-2411-184: Our experiments are guided by the following questions: (1) What is the advantage of our split scene representation compared with alternative representations? (2) What is the importance of the graph formulation and why do we differentiate types of edges with a heterogenous st-graph? (3) What is the importance of HH and RH attention in \modelName? (4) What are the failure cases of our method?.

elements.sent-2411-185: We conduct simulation experiments in random environment in Fig.~\ref{fig:simulator (a) developed with PyBullet~\cite{coumans2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-186: The robot, humans, and static obstacles are in a 12\,m$\times$12\,m arena.
elements.sent-2411-187: In each episode, rectangular obstacles are initialized with random shapes and random poses.
elements.sent-2411-188: The width and the length of each static obstacle are sampled from $\mathcal{N (1, 0.6^2)$ and are limited in $[0.1, 5]$ meters.}
elements.sent-2411-189: The initial positions of the humans and the robot are also randomized.
elements.sent-2411-190: The starting and goal positions of the robot are randomly sampled inside the arena.
elements.sent-2411-191: The distance between the robot's starting and the goal positions is between 5\,m and 6\,m.
elements.sent-2411-192: Some humans are dynamic and some are static.
elements.sent-2411-193: The goals of dynamic humans are set on the opposite side of their initial positions to create circle-crossing scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-194: In training, the number of humans varies from 5 to 9 and the number of static rectangular obstacles varies from 8 to 12.
elements.sent-2411-195: Among all humans, 0-2 of them are static and the rest are dynamic.
elements.sent-2411-196: In testing, the number of humans and obstacles are shown in the first column of Table~\ref{tab:baseline_results .}
elements.sent-2411-197: The heights of humans and obstacles are all above the height of the robot LiDAR to ensure detectability.
elements.sent-2411-198: To simulate a continuous human flow similar to \cite{liu2020decentralized,liu2023intention,xie2023drlvo , dynamic humans will move to new random goals immediately after they arrive at their goal positions or they get stuck in front of narrow passageways for more than 10 timesteps.}
elements.sent-2411-199: All dynamic humans are controlled by ORCA~\cite{van2011reciprocal .}
elements.sent-2411-200: This mixed setting prevents our network from learning an extremely aggressive policy in which the robot forces all humans to yield while achieving a high reward.
elements.sent-2411-201: Meanwhile, the simulation maintains enough reactive humans to resemble the real crowd behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-202: We use holonomic kinematics for humans.
elements.sent-2411-203: The preferred speed of humans ranges from 0.4\,m/s to 0.6\,m/s to accommodate the speed of the robot.
elements.sent-2411-204: We assume that humans can achieve the desired velocities immediately, and they will keep moving with these velocities for the next $\Delta t$ seconds.
elements.sent-2411-205: The simulation and control frequency $\Delta t$ is 0.1\,s.
elements.sent-2411-206: The maximum length of an episode is 491 timesteps or 49.1\,s.
elements.sent-2411-207: We now introduce the baselines and ablation models, training procedure, and evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-208: We compare the performance of our method with the following baselines:.
elements.sent-2411-209: Dynamic window approach (DWA)~\cite{fox1997dynamic searches for the optimal velocity that brings the robot to the goal with the maximum clearance from any obstacle.}
elements.sent-2411-210: DWA is a model-based method that only considers the current state.
elements.sent-2411-211: In addition, DWA represents both humans and obstacles as groups of small circles.
elements.sent-2411-212: With a map of the environment, $A^{*}$ is the global planner and generates 6 waypoints in the beginning of an episode.
elements.sent-2411-213: The inputs to the robot RL policy are a 2D LiDAR point cloud, the robot state, the waypoints, and the robot's final goal.
elements.sent-2411-214: No human detections are used and human features are included in the point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-215: The point cloud is passed through a CNN local planner, whose output is concatenated with the embedding of other inputs.
elements.sent-2411-216: In addition to the reward function in Eq.~\ref{eq:reward , the robot receives a small reward of 2 if it arrives at any waypoint in sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Decentrialized structural RNN (DS-RNN)~\cite{liu2020decentralized is an RL-based method that represents static obstacles as groups of small circles.}
elements.sent-2411-218: In network architecture, DS-RNN only contains the RH attention and the GRU.
elements.sent-2411-219: Homogeneous graph attention network (\homoBaseline)~\cite{velickovic2018graph is RL-based and splits human and obstacle inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-220: However, \homoBaseline does not differentiate between 3 types of nodes and 3 types of edges in policy network.
elements.sent-2411-221: Instead, \homoBaseline uses a single self-attention network to weigh humans, the robot, and the obstacle point cloud and feed the weighted sum of all embeddings into a GRU.
elements.sent-2411-222: In summary, DWA, \astarCNN, and DS-RNN mix humans and obstacles in both input representations and navigation algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-223: To show the benefits of each attention network, we perform an ablation study on the two attention models.
elements.sent-2411-224: The ablated models are defined as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-225: No attn: Both RH and HH attention networks are removed.
elements.sent-2411-226: No attn model only has the embedding layers for the inputs and the GRU.
elements.sent-2411-227: RH: The HH attention network is removed and the humans are weighted only once w.r.t.. the robot.
elements.sent-2411-228: Everything else is the same as ours.
elements.sent-2411-229: HH: The RH attention network is removed and the humans are weighted only once w.r.t.. other humans.
elements.sent-2411-230: RH+HH (ours): The full network as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:st (b).}
elements.sent-2411-231: We train all RL methods, including all baseines and ablations except DWA, for $2\times 10^8$ timesteps with a learning rate $ 5\times 10^{-5}$.
elements.sent-2411-232: The learning rate decays at a linear rate with respect to training timesteps.
elements.sent-2411-233: To accelerate and stabilize training, we run $28$ parallel environments to collect the robot's experiences.
elements.sent-2411-234: At each policy update, 30 steps of 6 episodes are used.
elements.sent-2411-235: We train and test all methods in a commercial desktop computer with an Intel Core i9-13900K processor, 32 GB memory, and a NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-236: Training our method takes approximately 48 hours.
elements.sent-2411-237: We test all methods with the same $500$ random unseen test episodes.
elements.sent-2411-238: For RL-based methods, we test the last 5 checkpoints (equivalent to the last 6000 training steps) and report the result of the checkpoint with the highest success rate.
elements.sent-2411-239: We conduct the testing in 5 different human and obstacle densities, as shown in the first columns of Table~\ref{tab:baseline_results and Table~\ref{tab:ablation_results .}}
elements.sent-2411-240: The first set of densities is the same as the training environment and is thus in training distribution.
elements.sent-2411-241: To test the generalization of methods, we change the range of human or obstacle numbers in the remaining four environments to values that do not overlap with those used in training.
elements.sent-2411-243: All testing scenarios, including those in the training distribution, are unseen during training.
elements.sent-2411-244: This is because in each testing episode, a new seed that is not used in training determines the number of humans and obstacles, the starting and goal positions of all agents, and the size and pose of obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-245: Only the 4 arena walls do not change across different episodes.
elements.sent-2411-246: The testing metrics measure the quality of the navigation and include the success rate, collision rate with humans and obstacles, timeout rate, the average navigation time of successful episodes in seconds, and path length of the successful episodes in meters.

elements.sent-2411-344: In this section, we reflect on the key components of our framework, discuss the limitations of our approach, and propose directions for future research.
elements.sent-2411-345: To overcome sim2real gaps, the design of simulation pipelines needs to be guided by the constraints of hardware and environments in the real-world.
elements.sent-2411-346: On one hand, we determine the input representation of \modelName based on what could be obtained from sensors and off-the-shelf perception modules and how accurate they are.
elements.sent-2411-347: We find that intermediate features, such as detected human positions and processed point clouds, reduces sim2real gaps.
elements.sent-2411-348: On the other hand, we also ensure the consistency of the simulation and real-world, such as the robot action space, whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-349: These design choices allow our policy to generalize to different simulation environments and deploy to challenging real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-350: However, although we have minimized the sim2real gaps through real2sim, certain gaps still exist.
elements.sent-2411-351: In real-world experiments, the difficulty of the task is reduced and the agility of the robot policy is only partially transferred from simulation.
elements.sent-2411-352: To further align the simulation and the real-world, we plan to explore the following directions for future work: (1) developing a more natural pedestrian model to replace the ORCA humans in the simulator, (2) revising our pipeline to enable self-supervised RL fine-tuning in the real-world~\cite{Zhu2020The, chang2023data , (3) using a small amount of real-world data to automatically optimize the parameters of our simulator to match the real-world environment~\cite{du2021auto,lim2022real2sim2real .}}
elements.sent-2411-353: A good scene representation is tailored to the needs of its downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-354: Besides the above sim2real considerations, our scene representation is split due the different nature of humans and obstacles for robot collision avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-355: The size of humans are small and their shapes are simple.
elements.sent-2411-356: Therefore, to avoid humans, the robot only needs to treat them as circles with a heading direction.
elements.sent-2411-357: In contrast, obstacles have larger and more complicated surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-358: The part of the obstacle contours that faces the robot is more important for collision avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-359: Therefore, point clouds are the most intuitive way to represent such useful information about obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-360: Our experiments in Sec.~\ref{sec:sim_exp show that this split scene representation reduces robot collision avoidance with both dynamic and static obstacles.}
elements.sent-2411-361: A side effect of our scene abstraction is the loss of detailed information such as gaits of humans and 3D shapes of obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-362: However, we argue that this is a tradeoff to minimize sim2real gaps with limited simulation tools and computation resources.
elements.sent-2411-363: Another side effect is the cascading errors between the perception modules and robot policy, such as inaccurate human detections or robot localization.

elements.sent-2411-365: Model-based approaches require low-fidelity data yet heavily rely on assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-366: In contrast, end-to-end learning approaches need few assumptions yet require high-fidelity data.
elements.sent-2411-367: Our structured learning method combines the best of both worlds: It requires low-fidelity data yet relies on minimal assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-368: By injecting structures into the neural network, we decompose a complex problem into smaller and relatively independent components.
elements.sent-2411-369: Note that our decomposition does not break the gradient flow, which keeps \modelName end-to-end trainable.
elements.sent-2411-370: We propose a principled way for network architecture design, increasing the transparency of these black-boxes.
elements.sent-2411-371: Our experiments demonstrate that the structured network outperforms both model-based methods and RL-based methods without structures, which empirically proves the effectiveness of structure learning for interactive tasks with multiple heterogeneous participants.
elements.sent-2411-372: Deep RL enables the robot to explore the environment and learn meaningful behaviors through trial and error.
elements.sent-2411-373: Without heuristics or demonstrations, the robot has to collect reward signals to improve its policy.
elements.sent-2411-374: Consequently, simulation development with a randomization scheme and a good reward design are indispensable to the performance of our method.

elements.sent-2411-377: In this article, we proposed HEIGHT, a novel structured graph network architecture for autonomous robot navigation in dynamic and constrained environments.
elements.sent-2411-378: Our approach takes advantage of the graphical nature and decomposability of the constrained crowd navigation problem, introducing the following two key novelties.
elements.sent-2411-379: First, we split and process the human and obstacle representations separately.
elements.sent-2411-380: This allows the robot to effectively reason about the different geometrics and dynamics of humans and obstacles, improving its ability to navigate complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-381: Second, we propose a heterogeneous st-graph to capture various types of interactions among the robot, humans, and obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-382: The decomposition of the scenario as a \hstGraph guides the design of the HEIGHT network with attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-383: Attention enables the robot to reason about the relative importance of each pairwise interaction, leading to adaptive and agile robot decision-making during navigation.
elements.sent-2411-384: Our simulation experiments show that the HEIGHT model outperforms traditional model-based methods and other learning-based methods in terms of collision avoidance and navigation efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-385: The HEIGHT model also demonstrates improved generalization in environments with varied human and obstacle densities.
elements.sent-2411-386: In real-world environments, HEIGHT is seamlessly transferred from simulation to everyday indoor navigation scenarios without additional training, showcasting its robustness and ability to overcome the sim-to-real gap.
elements.sent-2411-387: Our work suggests that reasoning about subtle spatio-temporal interactions is an essential step toward smooth human-robot interaction.
elements.sent-2411-388: Furthermore, our work highlights the significance of uncovering the inherent structure of complex problems and injecting these structures into learning frameworks to solve the problems in a principled manner.

elements.sent-2411-400: In this article, we study robot navigation to a destination without colliding with humans and obstacles, a crucial ability for applications such as last-mile delivery and household robots.

elements.sent-2411-403: Rising to these challenges, previous works have explored various approaches for robot crowd navigation in constrained spaces~\cite{fox1997dynamic,van2008reciprocal,chen2019crowd .}
elements.sent-2411-404: However, these works typically have one of two limitations: (1) They assume agents move in an open space without obstacles~\cite{chen2017decentralized,chen2019crowd,yang2023st2 ; (2) Their scene representations or navigation algorithms do not differentiate between dynamic and static obstacles, and thus the robot has difficulties taking adaptive strategies to avoid collisions~\cite{fox1997dynamic,chen2019relational,perez2021robot,liu2020decentralized .}}
elements.sent-2411-405: To address these limitations, we ask the following research question: How can a robot navigation policy represent and reason about diverse interactions in crowded and constrained environments to adaptively avoid collisions? To answer this question, we propose a framework that leverages the heterogeneity of interactions in crowded and constrained scenarios in both scene representation and navigation policy design.

elements.sent-2411-446: We use a 2D point cloud as the obstacle representation, which offers the following two variants.
elements.sent-2411-447: The first variant uses a real-time 2D point cloud obtained from onboard sensors.
elements.sent-2411-448: The second variant constructs an ``artificial'' point cloud which is computed from a pre-built map and the robot’s localization within that map.
elements.sent-2411-449: In this case, the point cloud is generated by raycasting from the robot’s estimated pose on the map, representing only static obstacles and unaffected by dynamic elements such as humans.
elements.sent-2411-450: The first variant reflects the robot’s instantaneous perception of its surroundings but requires a high-fidelity simulation of LiDAR data.
elements.sent-2411-451: The second variant is more robust to occlusions, imperfect human models, and sensor noise, but assumes the availability of an accurate map and reliable localization.
elements.sent-2411-452: In this work, we adopt the artificial point cloud variant to improve sim-to-real transferability.

elements.sent-2411-478: Lessons learned: To overcome sim2real gaps, the design of simulation pipelines needs to be guided by the constraints of hardware and environments in the real-world.
elements.sent-2411-479: Limitation and future work: However, although we have minimized the sim2real gaps through real2sim, certain gaps still exist.
elements.sent-2411-480: Before sim2real transfer, the policy needs to be finetined in simulations that mimic the target environments (Fig.\ref{fig:simulator (d) and (e)).}
elements.sent-2411-481: Despite this, in real-world experiments, the difficulty of the task is reduced and the agility of the robot policy is only partially transferred from simulation.
elements.sent-2411-482: To further align the simulation and the real-world, we plan to explore the following directions for future work: (1) developing a more natural pedestrian model to replace the ORCA humans in the simulator, (2) revising our pipeline to enable failure detections and self-supervised RL fine-tuning in the real-world~\cite{Zhu2020The, chang2023data , (3) using a small amount of real-world data to automatically optimize the parameters of our simulator to match the real-world environment~\cite{du2021auto,lim2022real2sim2real .}}
elements.sent-2411-483: Lessons learned: A good scene representation should be tailored to the needs of its downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-484: Limitation and future work: A side effect of our scene abstraction is the loss of detailed information such as gaits of humans and 3D shapes of obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-485: A future direction to automate the design of inputs and outputs is the joint optimization of the whole robotic stack from perception to control~\cite{hu2023_uniad,wang2024mosaic .}
elements.sent-2411-486: Lessons learned: Model-based approaches require low-fidelity data yet heavily rely on assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-487: Limitation and future work: The structured neural network utilizes the inductive bias behind human behaviors to solve specific problems.
elements.sent-2411-488: However, without re-training, the aforementioned network may not generalize to other human-robot interaction (HRI) scenarios such as autonomous driving.

elements.sent-2411-491: Lessons learned: Deep RL enables the robot to explore the environment and learn meaningful behaviors through trial and error.
elements.sent-2411-492: Limitation and future work: Nevertheless, our method is subjective to the inherent limitations of RL, such as the need to build simulations, low training data efficiency, and difficulties in long-horizon tasks.
